hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The properties at 7 and 9 Kensington Avenue and 560 Dundas Street also have value for the modifications made to accommodate a commercial conversion through a one-storey commercial addition. With its visibly layered built form, each of these properties is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as a row of five buildings, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features, including cross-gabled rooflines, projecting bays, scallop clad gables, and flat headed fenestration with stone lintels and sills. 560 Dundas Street West and 9 Kensington Avenue, which bookend the row, also retain their decorative gable brackets while 3 and 5 Kensington Avenue have retained the projecting bay with window at the first storey. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area. With its visible layering of residential and commercial built forms, each property is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area. Summary: Reference Primary Address Entrance Address(es) Number 167. 3 Kensington Avenue N/A 169. 5 Kensington Avenue N/A 171. 7 Kensington Avenue N/A 173. 9 Kensington Avenue N/A 144. 560 Dundas Street West 556, 558, 558A, 560A Dundas Street West 103 DCH | FO :D FO YTIC 5202 4 & 6 Kensington Avenue (L-R) 4 & 6 KENSINGTON AVENUE

photo_observations.storefront_description: left end: Chinese restaurant/shop with Chinese characters on sign (same buildings as IMG_20260320_012017528_HDR.jpg); centre: residential or mixed use with patio umbrella and outdoor seating behind metal railing; right: 'People Hair Salon' with Chinese characters; graffiti on lower portion
photo_observations.signage_observed: Chinese characters on left storefront; 'People Hair Salon' with Chinese characters at right; graffiti
photo_observations.facade_analysis_address: 7 Kensington Ave (Dreamland Hair Salon) / 5-3 Kensington / Side of 560 Dundas St (People Hair Salon)
photo_observations.brick_colour_observed: red-brown brick, consistent warm tone across row
photo_observations.condition_observed: fair; original Victorian bay-and-gable form well-preserved overall; various degrees of commercial modification at ground level; one unit appears to have outdoor patio seating; some graffiti visible at right end

windows_observed.gable: small windows visible in gable peaks of each unit
windows_observed.upper_floor: 2 windows per unit; white/light frames; appear to be 1-over-1 double-hung; some with blinds or curtains

photo_observations.doors_observed: multiple commercial and residential entrances; one residential door clearly visible (wood, panelled, painted light colour)
photo_observations.roof_type_observed: gable; 4+ steep front-facing gables in continuous row; typical bay-and-gable Victorian row; approximately 45-degree pitch; asphalt shingle roofing
photo_observations.decorative_elements_observed: classic bay-and-gable Victorian form; white-painted bargeboard at gable peaks; bay window projections at second floor; some buildings have enclosed second-floor porches/balconies; decorative woodwork at gable apex; some original trim preserved; string course visible between floors on some units
photo_observations.party_walls_observed: continuous row housing; party walls throughout; yellow fire hydrant visible at right

facade_description: 7 Kensington Ave is a house-form, converted house-form building. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its house-form, converted house-form typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The visible opening pattern is organized as 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double hung, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include string courses, gable brackets.
heritage_summary: Constructed as a row of five buildings, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features, including cross-gabled rooflines, projecting bays, scallop clad gables, and flat headed fenestration with stone lintels and sills. 560 Dundas Street West and 9 Kensington Avenue, which bookend the row, also retain their decorative gable brackets while 3 and 5 Kensington Avenue have retained the projecting bay with window at the first storey.
